good to see y'all my name is Alexei

we're very excited that we got a chance

would like to introduce you our last

fellow he is very well known in the

has been the University of Stanford
University of Montreal open the eye he
is a lead author of the very good book
that is available to you online on deep
learning so please take a time to to
learn from that as well as from today's
talk
thank you very much subdued thank you
good afternoon and thank you for coming
can everybody hear me okay in the back
Mike's not enough ok great so I will be
telling you all about the dark arts and
how to defend against them specifically
I'll be telling you all the things that
people can do to make machine learning
go wrong intentionally in potentially
dangerous ways and I'll tell you about
the state of the art and research to
defend against these kinds of attacks
that people can mount interfere with
machine learning I'm going you're an
overview of a field I'm not just telling
you about my own research some of this
work is mine some of this work is that
of my collaborators but probably the
majority of what I'll tell you about
today is actually other people's work if
you go to WWE and Goodfellow calm you
can get the slide so this present age
and the line that's underlined right
there is a hyperlink to a Google Doc
with a bibliography saying all the
different papers that I synthesized to
make this talk in order to make the
focus of the talk be on the concepts
rather than on which papers introduced
each of the concepts I'm not going to
tell you exactly which paper each slide
is drawing from necessarily but if you
look at the bibliography you can find it
if we look at the pipeline that we use
for a machine learning algorithm there
are several different stages to this
pipeline and from the point of view of
someone who's interested in security we
can think about ways that attackers can
strike each of these different stages
I'll use the same notation throughout
the talk basically it's the same
notation as in the deep learning book or
a drawings lecture notes if you're
familiar with either of those I'm going
to use a capital letter X to represent
the training data and then I'm going to
use the vector theta to represent
parameters learned by an algorithm that
we apply to that training data and then
at test time I'll use a lowercase letter
X to represent a new input that the
system processes using the parameters
that it learned applying the parameters
theta to the test input X we get some
kind of test output maybe a prediction
of which category or class X belongs to
as our test output Y hat in some cases
like reinforcement learning the test
output will be more complicated than
just a selection of a class but as far
as the security concerns go the division
of the pipeline is still relatively
similar you can interfere at training
time you can interfere at test time one
of the main concerns that we have is
making sure that the privacy of the
training data is preserved you can
imagine that if we use a little white
Queen to represent one player in a game
and a little black queen from chess to
represent another player in a game you
can imagine two players competing one
trying to learn useful functions from
data and publish information about those
functions and another player trying to
study those the the published results
and reveal private information about the
training set so here we begin with a
training set we would like to study it
and learn something useful that we
code in the parameters theta of a model
this might be something like private
medical data that we then used to learn
to make a diagnostic model and maybe we
would like to open-source that
diagnostic model and publish it so that
doctors around the world can use it to
diagnose their patients it would be
unfortunate if someone malicious could
examine this trained diagnostic model
and recover specific information about
individual people who are in the
training set
especially if any of that information
was personally identifiable and a lot of
the time information can be personally
identifiable even when even when you
think you might have taken some steps to
reduce its identifiability the main way
that we combat this kind of attack is
using what's called differential privacy
differential privacy is a concept that
was developed much earlier for
protecting accesses to databases we can
also apply it to machine learning the
basic idea is hinging on the word
differential we're looking at the
difference between what happens when we
try not to different data sets I think
most people in the audience will hear
differential and assume it's about
derivatives it's actually about
differences between two different runs
of a training algorithm one of these
runs happens on a training set with all
of the users in the data set the other
one happens on a data set where one user
has been removed if you can prove that
the outcome of the training process is
indistinguishable between these two
cases then we know that this one user
who is in one of the training sets and
out of the other training set had no
real effect on what the classifier
learns it means that the machine
learning model has learned general
patterns that could be useful for
example to diagnose a disease without
memorizing private information about the
individual user to do this of course the
machine learning algorithm has to be
somewhat randomized there has to be a
little bit of uncertainty about which
parameters that learns for a specific
training set otherwise you could invert
the process and and figure out exactly
which turning said it had looked at but
by introducing some randomness we can
guarantee that the machine learning
algorithm will produce statistically
indistinguishable outcomes regardless of
whether any individual user is in the
training set at Google brain in
collaboration with
pinette I we wrote a paper about a
technique that they call private
aggregation of teacher ensembles the
basic idea is that we can guarantee
differential privacy by splitting the
training data into more than one
disjoint group if a different model
trained on each of these two disjoint
groups learns the same pattern then we
know that it's safe to publish that
pattern that's the basic idea behind
this paté algorithm at the end of the
process we actually distill the
knowledge into the different in the
different teachers into a single student
network so rather than publishing the
individual teachers each one of which
may have memorized something about
specific specific users in the training
set we can publish the student network
who has learned a privatised summary of
the general patterns that the teachers
have found that they all agree on with
each other another important category of
security attacks that we could see in
machine learning is called training set
poisoning here the black queen
representing the malicious player in
this two-player game is able to
interfere with the training set
represented by the fact that they've
turned the capital X representing our
training data red this is called
poisoning because the effect on that
training set is then consumed by the
training process and affects everything
that comes afterward even though the
attacker can't directly affect the
parameters that the model learns the
training set can affect those parameters
and then the change in those parameters
changes the output that the model
produces at the end of the day so
imagine that we train a classifier
that's designed to recognize malware we
usually train these classifiers by
finding examples of malware in the wild
and adding them to our training set if
an attacker were particularly skilled at
training set poisoning they could make a
piece of decoy malware that they release
into the wild intending for us to catch
it at a day to edit to our training set
if they knew that it would then affect
the parameters theta that we learned in
a way that would allow some later real
piece of malware to bypass our filter by
injecting something that they control
into our training set they could
actually control the way that we predict
the output value for specific test
inputs later on
a recent paper that demonstrates
training set poisoning at relatively
large scale is this paper from Stanford
where they show that if they add this
image of a dog with some adversary
calculated pattern added to it and they
label it as a fish in the training set
then they can change the test set label
of all five of the dogs shown in the
lower row of this slide each of these
dogs was correctly classified as a dog
before the addition of this poisoned
training example after the addition of
the poisons training example they are
all recognized as being fish you could
imagine a similar thing could be done
with a malware binary that could cause
several malicious binaries that would be
recognized as dangerous to flip and be
recognized as safe and get through the
filter adversarial examples are another
kind of machine learning security
concern in this case the attacker is
able to interfere with the input at test
time and rather than presenting a normal
input they present an input that has
been optimized in order to fool the
model into producing an incorrect output
another version of security and privacy
concerns that come up in machine
learning is called model theft model
theft is when a malicious adversary is
able to observe inputs that are sent to
the model at test time and also to
observe the output of the model if they
can observe a lot of these input and
output pairs they can essentially build
their own training set and train their
own machine learning model that imitates
the behavior of the targeted model there
are a few different reasons that you may
not want a malicious adversary to be
able to do this one is as I just
mentioned you can you can recover
unwanted information about the training
set if you're able to recover the
parameters so earlier when I was
describing differential privacy and how
we would want to make sure that an
adversary could not analyze the
parameters of the model we need to also
keep in mind that while we may not
explicitly publish the parameters of the
model we publish the models predictions
and if those can be used to
reverse-engineer the parameters they can
be used to
Minh also be used to reverse engineer
the training set so model theft has
implications for privacy also once the
attacker knows exactly how the model
works they can make better adversarial
examples they can make maliciously
optimised inputs that are designed to
fool the model and after they've stolen
the model and reverse engineered it they
know exactly how the model behaves so
they can actually test their adversary
examples before they deploy them and be
confident that they're going to cause
the model to make a mistake one thing
that we've started to see is the very
advanced models can infer information
that we usually think of as private one
study found that a machine learning
model that is able to see a user's likes
on social media given enough likes can
actually predict attributes of that
users personality better than their
friends co-workers or family members can
do this reminds me a little bit of
Sherlock Holmes I've put a little
graphic representing Sherlock on the
left if you read the Sherlock Holmes
stories there's several instances of
encounters where someone comes to talk
to Sherlock Holmes and he guesses their
name and you know guesses that they're
having some conflict with their spouse
or something like that and his client is
always surprised that Sherlock already
knows their problem before they've told
it to him and then he explains how he
was able to deduce their problem by
little clues like marks on their shoes
and so on machine learning is kind of
like the modern-day version of Sherlock
Holmes it is able to infer things that
we don't realize we're broadcasting to
the world because it is able to
recognize patterns much better than the
average person and so in some cases
while we may technically be preserving
privacy in terms of what we publish our
machine learning algorithms may be able
to infer more than the information we
think we're able to publish another
security concern of seen is called
automated crowd turfing this is where
machine learning is actually used to
mount an attack for example in this
paper machine learning was used to
generate several fake Yelp reviews the
Yelp reviews are designed to look
realistic as if they were written by
real human users but you can imagine
that a malicious restaurant could use a
generative model to flood Yelp with
positive reviews for their arrest
that trick people into eating there even
though it's mediocre you can also
imagine flooding your competitors with
negative reviews that appear to be
realistic fake news is fortunately not
yet really something that happens with
machine learning as far as we know but a
lot of the techniques that we have today
have raised concern that they could be
used to generate fake photographic or
audio or video evidence of events that
have never happened in the future if you
go to future of fake news comm there's
this fake video of a speech by Obama
that never happened the man in the upper
left is using Obama's face as a neural
puppet as the man on the upper left
speaks the Obama's face matches his
facial expression and the words that
Obama has been saying in the audio are
also produced by a machine learning
model this seems to suggest that in the
future we won't be able to really a lot
rely on media evidence to tell us which
events have actually occurred anymore or
we will need to develop much better
machine learning forensics to detect
tampering with these kinds of evidence
machine learning models have also been
used for a password guessing essentially
users cannot really be trusted to
generate random passwords very well you
should use a password generator and
enable two-factor authentication for any
important account that you want to
secure machine learning models are able
to generate random passwords that
resemble the kind of passwords that
human users create they're able to
generate lists of passwords longer than
the ones that are already available on
the web for attackers to use longer than
the ones that have been scraped from
compromised users accounts and when you
use this additional lengthened list
provided by the machine learning model
you can break into accounts that you
would not be able to break into
otherwise some people have even
suggested that AI may have concerns for
geopolitics in general Elon Musk and
Vladimir Putin have both said that in
the future AI will actually be used for
a conflict between states so a lot of
these concerns about machine learning
security could in the future have
concerns for
national security and a lot of what
motivates me and my research is to make
sure that we make it harder to attack
than to defend so that machine learning
can promote stability of interactions
between different entities who may want
to have a conflict most of the remainder
of the talk will be a deep dive into
adversarial examples the aspect of
machine learning security that I've
spent most of my own time studying
adversarial examples are inputs that
cause machine learning models to make a
mistake and nobody studied them until
very recently because until very
recently machine learning models made
mistakes all the time in 2011 if you had
come to me and said hey and I've got an
example that makes a classifier produce
the wrong output I would say good for
you I have about 5,000 of those you know
in in my terminal right now but since
about 2013 we've started to see a lot of
different tasks like object detection
object recognition fake face recognition
reading obfuscated text for CAPTCHAs
reading reading photos of text and so on
where machine learning algorithms have
actually matched or exceeded human
performance in some cases there's kind
of a big asterisk
like on an image net humans probably are
better at computer vision than machine
learning models on the image net data
set we test both humans and machines by
asking them to recognize a thousand
different categories and you know
there's a lot of South American
rainforest animals that most of us are
not very good at recognizing because
you've never heard of them before but
the machine learning algorithm beats us
because it has a training set with a
thousand images of every category
modular those asterisks machine learning
works pretty well now and so it's become
interesting to study the cases where it
fails adversarial examples are one of
the cases where machine learning fails
very consistently in this particular
example here I've started with an image
of a panda on the left and I've added a
carefully calculated vector in the
middle to this image I've scaled down
the magnitude of the vector that I added
to the point where you can you can just
basically not detect the perturbation if
you represent this image in 32-bit
floating point the perturbation is there
if you represent it with 8-bit in
it gets rounded down to nothing so as
you look at this image on the projector
the panel on the left and the pen on the
right are actually exactly the same as
each other for the inception
convolutional Network used to recognize
this image they are in fact different
because the network accepts inputs in
float32 format the image on the left is
recognized as being a panda with about
70 percent confidence the image on the
right is incorrectly recognized as being
a Gibbon with about 99.9 percent
confidence so not only have we made the
model make a mistake we've actually made
it have higher confidence when it's
wrong than when it was right this
phenomenon for neural nets was
discovered back sometime before 2013
first published in 2013 I was on a team
that published it with Christians egg ad
being the first person who really
noticed this independently Batista
Biggio also noticed this for things like
amnesty law sapphires and published that
in the same year so there were two
groups independently studying this
phenomenon in 2014 I developed a faster
cheaper way of making these fooling
images and that's the kind that I've
actually shown on this slide here so
that's that's done in a closed form
formula there's no iterative
optimization required to turn this panda
into a Gibbon and on these kinds of
images machine learning models in many
cases get essentially 100 percent
accuracy the every now and then your
optimizer might fail but it's really
just noise when christian first noticed
this effect he was actually trying to
understand how convolutional networks
work he wasn't trying to break them he
was thinking what would happen if I ran
gradient a sent on the blog probability
of the airplane class and I've repeated
the same kind of experiment he did here
each of these little grids is an

not that it's just barely an airplane
it's it's you know ninety percent
confident that it's an air
if you're anything like me you look at

you must have a bug in your gradient

the first time I ran this I took the

this is really happening
but essentially if you go and you
optimize inputs to neural Nets you can
change the class while barely making any

things happen to linear models as well
this is a problem for most of machine
learning not just for neural nets in
this case I have a 10 class softmax

and complicated datasets
so here I've just run it on the eminence

as you follow the animation left to

classes everywhere that you see a yellow

all the other classes until at the
bottom right we're back to the 9 class
the only image that I would say is
correctly classified here is the image
of the 9 at the bottom right and the
only reason that that is correctly
classified is that we started the whole
process with a 9 so we managed to use
gradient descent to visit all of the
different classes without ever really
meaningfully changing the input and this
isn't something resulting from the model
being extremely complicated this is just
a linear model so adversarial examples
are a pretty salient phenomenon and they
happen for lots of different kinds of
machine learning models even very simple
ones so what's actually going on here
when Christian and my other co-authors
and I first started studying adversarial
examples we all kind of assumed that
they were an instance of overfitting in
some sense
so the overfitting story i've tried to
illustrate with a training set
containing a line of blue X's and a
curve of Green Oaks
the blue X's are one class that green

probability mess showing where a

examples from each of those classes
would appear according to the

set blue X's only managed to put a blob

O's but then we also just throw out some

class is so big and complicated and our

random because we're choosing from a

according to this story some of those
random blobs would have adversarial
examples in them so I've drawn a red X

that she probably classified as a No
well it seems like a reasonable story if
we go out and start gathering some

some things you might do are we might
try running the same adversarial example
through several different models if we
really are just suffering from bad luck
and every model learns in a more or less

process when it chooses a function from
hypothesis space we would expect
different models to overfit differently
and suffer from different adversarial
examples that's not actually what we
found we found that the same adversarial
example fooled several different models
that led me to think that something
different might be going on that we
might actually be seeing adversarial
examples coming from the model being too
linear here I've drawn the same kind of

linear classifier dividing up space we
see here as you can still draw
adversarial examples even for this very
simple decision boundary in particular
modern deep networks are very piecewise
linear and we do this because we've
actually designed the hidden units of
the networks to be linear in order to
make them easier to train for example
rectified linear units
are essentially piecewise-linear with
two pieces but what happens if you
actually make a network out of them if
you're like me I always assumed that the
network structure was like combining a
bunch of Lego blocks to make a really
complicated shape that didn't look very
much like a piecewise linear function
anymore so I tried making linear
cross-sections moving in a straight line
through image space and plotting the
outputs of the neural net on the left I
show you different images that lie along
a Ray in RN and on the right I show you
a plot tracing out the unnormalized log
probabilities produced by the model
these are the values right before the
softmax at the end and every different
color of curve is a different class for
a CF our ten classifier you can see that
the top probability in most places is
the Frog class this happens anytime that
we've strayed very far from the training
data on the left side of the curve the
Frog class is high when we've subtracted
this perturbation off of the input and
on the right side of the curve the Frog
class is high when we've added this
perturbation on to the input the only
place where you actually get the right
class where the where the automobile
curve comes up higher is in the center
of the plot where there's no
perturbation but as you move further out
the model extrapolates in a basically
linear fashion so it seems like the
adversary is able to exploit this linear
behavior of the model they know that if
they make a small change locally and
they get a small response they can make
a very large change in the same
direction and they'll get a
proportionately larger response so they
can actually predict where they should
go looking to get an input that looks
like a frog one comment I should make
really quick is I'm saying that neural
nets are very linear as a function of
their input if you look at what a really
nut looks like as a function of its
input you basically have a bunch of
matrices multiplied together with some
real lose gating them you can represent
those rows locally as being diagonal
matrices with zeros or ones on the
diagonal if you look at neural notes as
a function of their parameters they
really are more nonlinear there's a
paper by Andrew Sachs called
Solutions to the exact dynamics of
learning where he shows that even a
neural net with no nonlinear hidden
units actually has a very nonlinear
mapping from weights to cost function
values and that that's sufficient to
describe a lot of the behavior receive
and retrain neural nets but for an
adversary who wants to find malicious
inputs and they're dealing with a
different mapping they're dealing with
the mapping from inputs to outputs and
that mapping turns out to be much
simpler than the mapping from parameters
to outputs if we look at different
classes we see that they're actually
very close in l2 distance from each
other and the top row here I show the
nearest MN is 3 and the nearest m27 and
I show the difference between them the
difference between them has an l2 norm
of 3.96
if instead I make a random perturbation
with that same norm and apply that
perturbation in the middle row we get a
bunch of noise added to the three but
you can still definitely recognize it so
small perturbations per pixel are large
enough in l2 distance to travel all the
way between different classes that's
part of what's going on with Ed Bruce
Ehrlich samples if we change a lot of
pixels all those little tiny changes can
each vote for a specific class and if
they coordinate their efforts to all
that for the same class a lot of those
little tiny votes can add up to one very
big vote when we construct adversarial
examples we want to make sure that we
don't actually accidentally change the
true class if we loaded an image of a
panda and then we turned it into a
Gibbon it would be fair for the model to
say it's a given we want to make sure
that we actually keep the class of the
object the same from the start to the
finish so a lot of the time because
these l2 constraints need to be really
big to make sure we don't change the
class instead we'll use L infinity
constraints specifically we say no pixel
can change more than some small amount
Upsilon and that make sure that we can't
do things like erase the white ink of a
numbness digit and we know that whatever
perturbation we make will leave the same
object in the image after we've applied
the perturbation one very fast way that
we can make adversary examples is by
applying this observation that neural
nets are very linear as a function of
their input and by applying this
required
that no pixel should change by more than
epsilon the observation that the neural
net is linear tells us that we can get
away with making a Taylor series
approximation of the cost of the neural
net pays when it classifies the input so
we essentially linearized the cost
around the current input and then we
maximize that approximate cost subject
to this constraint that no pixel should
change by more than some amount Upsilon
and that gives us a very fast rule that
we can apply we just take the gradient
of the cost with respect to the input we
take the sign of the gradient at each
pixel and we add epsilon times that sign
at the gradient to the input so the sign
is basically telling us do we hurt the
model when we increase this pixel or
decrease this pixel if it hurts the
model to increase the pixel we add
Upsilon to the pixel if it hurts the
model to decrease the pixel we subtract
epsilon off of the pixel it's a very
straightforward rule and unless you've
intentionally designed your model to
resist this kind of attack it will
actually fool most machine learning
models very reliably if we then make
maps of how adversarial examples are
spread throughout space we can start to
answer some basic questions like our
adversarial examples rare little points
that you have to search for very
carefully or do they live in very dense
spaces where you can wander around in
that space and find arbitrarily many
adversary examples our maps show us that
actually they live in these dense spaces
that look a lot like half spaces when we
make two dimensional cross-sections so
in each of these maps every little cell
is a different C for ten tests example
and the coordinates within that cell
represent different points and image
space the very center of the cell is a
clean sea for ten tests example as you
move right from the center we're adding
an adversarial perturbation as we move
left from the center we're subtracting
off that perturbation and then as you
move up or down we're adding noise or
subtracting noise the noise Direction is
there as a control so that you can see
that this doesn't happen for every
direction you might explore if the
correct class is produced when you run
the model and a point we label that
point with a white pixel in the map
otherwise we have a different unique
color for each of those see for
classes and replot the color of the
incorrect class that was chosen by the
model so everywhere you see white the
model has produced the right answer and
you'll see that it's white on the left
side of all of these plots and that
there's basically a vertical dividing
line as you move to the right we see all
of the other colors where the model has
picked other incorrect see far ten
classes so you can see that essentially
any perturbation that has high dot
product with the adversarial gradient
will cause the model to make a mistake
we're not trying to hit a bull's-eye on
a target that's a mile away we just need
to move in the right general direction
and we'll find a mistake the test data
happens to never move in these
directions but if we actually go looking
for them intentionally they're very easy
to find
Florian tremé and his collaborators at
Stanford decided to estimate the
dimensionality of these subspaces in
these plots you can see that there's at
least a two dimensional subspace you can
explore and find adversarial examples
everywhere within that subspace we found
that for for eminence at least there's
around a twenty dimensional subspace
that you can explore and every convex
